launch creates public exposure
errors would damage trust
the current version has unresolved risks
waiting is less costly than failure

The sentence compresses a reasoning structure.


4.8.5 The Sentence Does Not Determine the Required Response
A sentence may invite different responses.
For example:

“I am worried about this plan.”

The speaker may want:

reassurance
revision
discussion
evidence
delay
support
a decision

The listener must infer the expected response.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.9 The Role of Relevance
Listeners assume that speech is relevant.
When someone says something, the listener usually assumes there is a reason.
This assumption drives inference.
For example, imagine two people standing near an open window.
One says:

“It is cold.”

The listener does not treat this as random meteorological information.
The listener asks:

Why is this being said here and now?

Because the window is open, the sentence becomes relevant as an indirect request.
If the same sentence is spoken during a weather report, it has a different function.
Relevance connects language to situation.
A sentence becomes meaningful because the listener searches for why it matters now.
This is one reason communication can be efficient.
Speakers do not need to state every connection explicitly.
Listeners use relevance to reconstruct the intended meaning.
But this also creates risk.
If the listener identifies the wrong relevance, misunderstanding follows.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.10 The Role of Implication
Implication allows speakers to communicate more than they state.
For example:

“Some of the reports were accurate.”

This may imply:

Not all of the reports were accurate.

The speaker did not say this directly, but the listener may infer it.
Another example:

“He is very punctual.”

Said when discussing whether someone is qualified for a complex leadership role, this may imply that punctuality is the only positive thing the speaker can honestly mention.
The meaning comes not only from the sentence, but from what the sentence suggests in context.
Implication is powerful because it allows language to remain indirect while still carrying meaning.
But implication is also unstable.
Different listeners may infer different meanings.
This is why high-stakes communication often requires explicitness.
When misunderstanding would be costly, implication should be reduced.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.11 The Role of Tone
Tone changes meaning.
A sentence may have the same words but a different tone.

“I am fine.”

This may mean:

I am genuinely fine.
I am upset but do not want to discuss it.
I am angry and expect you to notice.
I am trying to end the conversation.
I am protecting myself.

Tone is not an optional layer.
It is part of how meaning is carried.
In written communication, tone may be signaled through:

word choice
punctuation
length
formality
response speed
formatting
emoji
hedging
directness
silence

For example:

“Okay.”

In one context, it may be neutral agreement.
In another, it may feel cold.
In another, it may signal resignation.
The sentence is minimal.
The tone carries much of the meaning.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.12 The Role of Silence
Meaning can also be produced by what is not said.
Silence is not always empty.
It may mean:

agreement
disagreement
hesitation
refusal
discomfort
lack of knowledge
respect
fear
resistance
strategic withholding

For example, in a meeting, if a manager proposes a plan and no one responds, the silence may have many possible meanings.
It may mean people agree.
It may mean they are confused.
It may mean they disagree but do not feel safe speaking.
It may mean the proposal is not important enough to discuss.
It may mean they need time to think.
The sentence is absent, but meaning is still present.
A pragmatic speaker must learn to interpret silence carefully.
Silence should not be overread, but it should not be ignored.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.13 Indirect Meaning
Indirect meaning is common in human communication.
People often use one form to perform another function.
A question may function as a request:

“Can you send me the file?”

A statement may function as a warning:

“The road is icy.”

A compliment may function as persuasion:

“You are good at explaining this.”

A description may function as criticism:

“The report is missing the conclusion.”

A suggestion may function as instruction:

“You might want to revise this section.”

Indirectness is not weakness.
It is a way of managing relationship, politeness, pressure, and social complexity.
However, indirect language depends heavily on shared context and inference.
When the listener does not infer correctly, indirectness fails.
Therefore, indirect language is useful when:

the relationship requires tact
the stakes allow some ambiguity
the listener has enough context
the speech act should be softened
directness would create unnecessary resistance

Direct language is better when:

action must be clear
responsibility must be explicit
time is limited
misunderstanding would be costly
the listener lacks background
the issue is operational or high-stakes

Good communication is not always direct.
Good communication is appropriately direct.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.14 Misunderstanding as Frame Mismatch
Many misunderstandings happen because speaker and listener operate with different frames.
The speaker says something under one assumed context.
The listener interprets it under another.
For example:

“We should be more careful next time.”

The speaker may intend:

“Let us improve the process.”

The listener may hear:

“You are blaming me.”

The sentence itself does not settle the matter.
The listener interprets through relationship history, emotional state, prior conflict, power difference, and perceived intention.
Another example:

“This is not ready.”

The speaker may mean:

“It needs more work before release.”

The listener may hear:

“Your work is poor.”

Again, the gap is not in grammar.
It is in context, identity, and inference.
To repair misunderstanding, one must often clarify the frame:

“I am not criticizing your effort. I am saying the current version does not yet meet the release standard.”

This sentence separates person, effort, object, and standard.
It reduces the risk of relational misinterpretation.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.15 Explicit Meaning and Implied Meaning
A useful distinction:








Type
Definition
Example




Explicit Meaning
What is directly stated
“The draft needs revision.”


Implied Meaning
What is suggested but not directly stated
“The draft is not yet acceptable.”


Inferred Meaning
What the listener concludes
“My work is being judged negatively.”


Intended Meaning
What the speaker meant to communicate
“Improve structure before submission.”


Received Meaning
What the listener actually understands
“The manager is disappointed in me.”



Communication succeeds when intended meaning and received meaning are aligned enough for the situation.
They do not need to be identical in every detail.
But when the gap is too large, communication fails.
This is why speakers must manage not only what they say, but what their words are likely to imply.
Listeners must also manage their inferences.
They should ask:

“Do you mean that the whole direction is wrong, or only that this section needs work?”

Clarifying inference is a major part of pragmatic competence.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.16 High-Context and Low-Context Communication
Some communication relies heavily on shared background and implication.
This can be called high-context communication.
Other communication makes more information explicit.
This can be called low-context communication.
High-context communication is efficient when people share:

history
norms
goals
roles
assumptions
emotional understanding
repeated routines

Example:

“Same as before.”

This may be enough inside a team with shared history.
Low-context communication is necessary when:

people do not share background
the stakes are high
the task is complex
the audience is broad
the message must travel across time
the speaker cannot rely on memory or relationship

Example:

“Use the same review process as last Friday: first check the structure, then verify evidence, then leave comments under each section, and send the final version before 5 p.m.”

Neither mode is superior in all cases.
High-context communication saves time but risks exclusion and misunderstanding.
Low-context communication improves clarity but may feel slow, formal, or excessive.
A competent speaker chooses the level of explicitness according to the situation.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
section: 4.17 The Compression and Expansion of Meaning
text:
4.17 The Compression and Expansion of Meaning
Language constantly compresses and expands meaning.
When speaker and listener share context, meaning can be compressed.
For example:

“The usual problem.”

This sentence may contain an entire history.
But when context is missing, meaning must be expanded.
The speaker must say:

“The issue is that the data pipeline fails whenever the input format changes, because our validation layer does not catch schema drift early enough.”

Expansion makes hidden structure visible.
This is essential in:

teaching
documentation
leadership
cross-team work
legal communication
technical explanation
conflict resolution
decision-making

In general:
More shared background → more compression possible
Less shared background → more expansion required
Higher stakes → more explicitness required
Lower stakes → more implication allowed
A major error in communication is using compressed language when expanded language is required.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.18 Meaning and Responsibility
Because actual meaning exceeds sentence form, speakers cannot always defend themselves by saying:

“I only said the words.”

Words have predictable implications in context.
A speaker is not responsible for every possible interpretation, but the speaker is responsible for reasonable interpretations.
For example:

“You people always do this.”

A speaker may claim they meant something harmless. But in many contexts, the phrase carries group blame and hostility.
Another example:

“I guess some people care about quality.”

This may literally refer to “some people,” but in context it may function as an indirect attack.
Pragmatic responsibility means understanding that language has force beyond literal form.
A responsible speaker asks:

What will this likely imply?
What inference am I inviting?
Could this reasonably be heard as blame, pressure, contempt, promise, refusal, or threat?
Do I need to make my intention explicit?
Do I need to reduce ambiguity?

Meaning is not fully controlled by the speaker.
But it is not random either.
The speaker must design language with likely interpretation in mind.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.19 Meaning and Repair
Because meaning depends on context and inference, misunderstanding is inevitable.
Repair is therefore part of communication.
Repair means adjusting language after noticing that intended meaning and received meaning have diverged.
Useful repair phrases include:
Let me clarify what I mean.
I do not mean X; I mean Y.
The main point is not A, but B.
I can see how that sounded like X.
What I intended to say was Y.
Let me separate the person from the issue.
Let me make the standard explicit.
Let me restate the request more concretely.
Repair is not failure.
It is a normal mechanism of situated communication.
In complex situations, the first sentence rarely carries the full intended meaning perfectly.
Meaning is often negotiated across turns.
The speaker says something.
The listener interprets.
The listener responds.
The speaker adjusts.
The shared meaning becomes more stable.
Communication is therefore not only transmission.
It is coordination.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
section: 4.20 Practical Method: Reading Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.20 Practical Method: Reading Beyond the Sentence
To understand actual meaning, ask six questions.

4.20.1 What is the literal sentence?
Start with the words.
Do not ignore the explicit content.
What did the speaker actually say?


4.20.2 What is the situation?
Place the sentence inside context.
Who said it, to whom, when, where, and under what stakes?


4.20.3 What background is assumed?
Identify shared knowledge.
What does the speaker assume the listener already knows?


4.20.4 What is being implied?
Look for unstated meaning.
What is suggested but not directly stated?


4.20.5 What action is invited?
Identify the speech function.
What does the speaker expect the listener to think, feel, say, or do?


4.20.6 What alternative interpretations exist?
Avoid premature certainty.
Could this mean something else in this situation?
These questions prevent both naive literalism and uncontrolled overinterpretation.
The goal is not to invent hidden meaning everywhere.
The goal is to infer responsibly from sentence, context, background, and situation.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
section: 4.21 Practical Method: Speaking Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.21 Practical Method: Speaking Beyond the Sentence
To speak effectively, design for actual meaning, not only sentence correctness.
Before important speech, ask:







Question
Purpose




What do I literally want to say?
Clarifies sentence content


What do I want the listener to understand?
Clarifies intended meaning


What might they infer?
Anticipates received meaning


What background do they have?
Determines explicitness


What is at stake?
Determines precision


What should be made direct?
Reduces dangerous ambiguity


What can remain implied?
Preserves efficiency or tact


What may need repair?
Prepares for clarification



Good speaking is not merely producing grammatical sentences.
It is designing the relationship between sentence form and expected interpretation.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
section: 4.22 Example: A Sentence in Three Contexts
text:
4.22 Example: A Sentence in Three Contexts
Consider:

“This is not what I expected.”


4.22.1 Context 1: Product Review
A client says this after seeing a prototype.
Meaning:

The deliverable does not match expectation.

Likely function:

Correction, dissatisfaction, renegotiation.

Needed response:

Clarify expectation, identify gap, define revision.



4.22.2 Context 2: Surprise Gift
A friend says this after receiving a gift.
Meaning may be positive:

I am surprised in a good way.

Likely function:

Emotional reaction.

Needed response:

Attend to tone and facial expression.



4.22.3 Context 3: Performance Evaluation
A manager says this to an employee.
Meaning:

The work may fall below standard.

Likely function:

Feedback or warning.

Needed response:

Ask for specific criteria and corrective direction.

The sentence is identical.
The actual meaning depends on situation, relationship, expectation, tone, and stakes.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.23 The Danger of Literalism
Literalism treats sentence meaning as the whole meaning.
Sometimes literalism is useful.
In legal, technical, or formal contexts, precise wording matters greatly.
But in ordinary human communication, strict literalism can miss the point.
If someone says:

“Do whatever you want.”

The literal meaning gives permission.
The actual meaning may be:

“I am upset and withdrawing from the conversation.”

If the listener responds only to the literal meaning, they may make the situation worse.
Another example:

“No, it is fine.”

Literal meaning:

It is acceptable.

Actual meaning may be:

It is not fine, but I do not want to argue.

This does not mean the listener should assume hidden resentment everywhere.
It means the listener should attend to context, tone, and pattern.
Literal meaning is the starting point, not the endpoint.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.24 The Danger of Overinterpretation
If literalism is one danger, overinterpretation is another.
Overinterpretation occurs when the listener invents more meaning than the evidence supports.
For example:

“Your draft needs more examples.”

A listener may infer:

“The speaker thinks I am incompetent.”

But the sentence may only mean:

“The draft needs more examples.”

Pragmatic competence requires balance.
A listener should neither ignore implication nor manufacture it without evidence.
A useful discipline is to separate:
What was said
What was implied
What I inferred
What I felt
What I need to clarify
This separation prevents emotional inference from becoming false certainty.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Meaning Beyond the Sentence
text:
4.25 Meaning as Cooperative Construction
Meaning is not produced by the speaker alone.
It is co-constructed.
The speaker designs an utterance.
The listener interprets it.
The context constrains possible meanings.
The relationship shapes interpretation.
The next response confirms, rejects, or modifies the meaning.
For example:
Speaker:

“The current version feels unfinished.”

Listener:

“Do you mean the writing, the structure, or the evidence?”

Speaker:

“Mainly the structure. The argument is there, but the sequence is hard to follow.”

Now the meaning becomes clearer.
This interaction shows that meaning can be refined.
Initial speech may be incomplete.
Clarifying questions expand it.
Shared understanding emerges through interaction.
In pragmatic language, understanding is often not instant.
It is negotiated.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space

title: 4  Options, Alternatives, and Decision Space
text:
4.4 Chapter Summary
This chapter has studied how language constructs options, alternatives, and decision space. Options are formed through language by naming possible actions and giving them boundary for evaluation. Alternatives require meaningful difference along structural dimensions. The false binary is one of the most common constraints on decision space, limiting thought to two poles when a wider field exists. Generating options requires deliberate expansion, while structuring them requires dimensional analysis and discipline.

4.4.1 Essential Knowledge
The following principles must be retained and applied whenever you face a decision.
First, options are not found but constructed through language. If you cannot name an option clearly, you cannot evaluate it. Second, always test for false binaries. When a situation appears to offer only two choices, ask: what third option combines elements of both, can the decision be staged, and can the options be redesigned? Third, a real alternative must differ along at least one structural dimension: action, resource, risk, timing, commitment, dependency, or value. If two options do not differ meaningfully, they are not real alternatives. Fourth, decision space can be expanded by relaxing assumed constraints, splitting decisions into stages, and adopting perspectives you do not initially hold. It can be focused by eliminating options that are not actionable, not meaningfully different, or not relevant to the goal. Fifth, the quality of your judgment is limited by the quality of your option set. A brilliant evaluation of a narrow or false set of options still produces a poor decision. Spend as much effort constructing the option space as you spend comparing the alternatives within it.
crumbs: Part I — Problems, Goals, and Decision Space
4  Options, Alternatives, and Decision Space

title: Part II — Comparison, Trade-Off, and Evaluation
text:
Comparison as Structured Seeing
This part studies what happens after options have entered the field of choice. Once alternatives are visible, language must do more than name them. It must compare them, separate their differences, expose their costs, and judge them under explicit criteria. This is where deliberate language moves from possibility to evaluation.
A person may have many options and still remain unable to decide. The difficulty is not always a lack of choice. Sometimes the difficulty is that the choices have not been made comparable. They sit beside each other as vague possibilities, but the mind has no stable dimensions along which to examine them.
Comparison gives structure to alternatives.
It asks:
Without comparison, alternatives remain a list. With comparison, they become a decision field.
To compare is not simply to say that one thing is better than another. Comparison begins by making difference visible.
Two options may differ in speed, cost, difficulty, reversibility, risk, emotional burden, long-term value, social consequence, or alignment with identity. If these dimensions remain implicit, people often argue at the wrong level. One person may care about safety while another cares about growth. One person may evaluate by short-term efficiency while another evaluates by long-term positioning. The conversation appears to be about the options, but the deeper disagreement is about the dimension of comparison.
Deliberate language makes the comparison dimension explicit.
It does not only say:
It asks:
This shift is essential. Evaluation without an explicit dimension becomes preference disguised as judgment. A person may say, “This is the best choice,” when what he actually means is, “This choice best satisfies the criterion I care about most.”
Comparison requires a frame. The same option can look strong or weak depending on the frame used to examine it.
An option may be better for speed but worse for quality.
It may be better for safety but worse for growth.
It may be better for short-term stability but worse for long-term transformation.
It may be better for individual freedom but worse for collective coordination.
Language must hold these differences without collapsing them too early.
crumbs: Part II — Comparison, Trade-Off, and Evaluation

title: Part II — Speech Action and Intention
text:
“I will send it tomorrow.”





“Could you close the door?”





“I am sorry.”
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: Part II — Speech Action and Intention
text:
From Meaning to Function
Part I examined the situation of speech and the way meaning exceeds the sentence.
Part II moves one step further.
It asks:

What is the speaker doing by saying this?

This question is central to pragmatic language.
A speech act cannot be understood only by analyzing its grammar. It must be understood by identifying its function within a situation.
The same sentence may perform different actions depending on intention, context, relationship, and stakes.
For example:

“You are here early.”

This may function as:

a simple observation
a compliment
a question
a suspicion
a criticism
an invitation to explain

The literal content remains stable.
The speech action changes.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: Part II — Speech Action and Intention
text:
Language as Action
Speech can perform many kinds of action.
It can:

give information
ask for information
request action
issue commands
refuse requests
make promises
express agreement
express disagreement
give permission
withdraw permission
assign responsibility
create obligation
repair damage
define social position

These actions are not secondary to language.
They are part of what language is for.
In human life, many important realities are created or changed through speech:

“I agree.”
“You are hired.”
“I quit.”
“I promise.”
“You are responsible for this.”
“The meeting is canceled.”
“We choose option B.”

Such sentences do not merely describe decisions.
They enact them.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: Part II — Speech Action and Intention
text:
Intention and Recognition
Speech action depends on intention, but intention alone is not enough.
For a speech act to work, the listener must usually recognize what the speaker is trying to do.
A request succeeds only if it is understood as a request.
A warning succeeds only if it is understood as a warning.
A promise succeeds only if it is understood as a promise.
An apology succeeds only if it is understood as an apology.
This means that speech action exists between speaker and listener.
It is not located only inside the speaker’s mind.
A speaker may intend to comfort, but the listener may hear condescension.
A speaker may intend to suggest, but the listener may hear command.
A speaker may intend to refuse gently, but the listener may hear uncertainty.
A speaker may intend to criticize the work, but the listener may hear an attack on the person.
The success of speech action depends on alignment between:
Intention → Expression → Recognition → Response
When this chain breaks, communication fails.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: Part II — Speech Action and Intention
text:
The Function of This Part
This part studies how utterances perform actions.
It focuses on several core speech functions:

informing
requesting
refusing
promising
positioning

These are not merely isolated speech types.
They are foundational operations of human interaction.
To inform is to change what another person knows.
To request is to invite or pressure another person to act.
To refuse is to set a boundary.
To promise is to create future obligation.
To position is to define oneself or others within a social field.
Each function has its own structure, risks, and responsibility.
A person who cannot request clearly will struggle to coordinate.
A person who cannot refuse clearly will lose boundaries.
A person who promises carelessly will damage trust.
A person who positions poorly will mismanage identity, authority, and relationship.
Speech action is therefore not only linguistic.
It is practical, social, and ethical.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: Part II — Speech Action and Intention
text:
Speech Acts and Responsibility
Once language is understood as action, speech becomes a matter of responsibility.
Words can create expectations.
Words can assign roles.
Words can invite trust.
Words can damage trust.
Words can open possibilities.
Words can close them.
A speaker is responsible not only for whether a sentence is grammatically correct, but for what the sentence does in context.
This is especially important when speech creates obligation or consequence.
For example:

“I will try.”

This may seem harmless.
But depending on the situation, it may be heard as:

a weak promise
a non-commitment
a polite refusal
an expression of uncertainty
a way to delay decision

If the stakes are high, such ambiguity can become costly.
Deliberate speech requires the speaker to know what action their words are performing.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: Part II — Speech Action and Intention
text:
Structural Logic of Part II
The movement of this part is:
Utterance
→ Function
→ Intention
→ Recognition
→ Obligation
→ Social Position
First, we examine how speech carries function beyond content.
Then we study specific speech actions: informing, requesting, refusing, and promising.
Finally, we examine positioning: how language places people within relationships, roles, authority structures, and social expectations.
The central claim is simple:

To speak is not only to say something.
To speak is to do something.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: Part II — Speech Action and Intention
text:
Transition
Once language is understood as action, the next question becomes more precise:

What kinds of action can speech perform?

The following chapters examine this question through the basic operations of pragmatic life: informing, requesting, refusing, promising, and positioning.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Comparing Alternatives
text:
5.1 From Options to Comparison
Once options have been constructed, the next task is comparison. A decision space may contain many alternatives, but they do not automatically become comparable; they may sit beside one another without yet being organized along shared dimensions. Language must make differences visible, stable, and discussable.
Comparison is not the same as judgment. Judgment says one option is better; comparison asks first: better in what way? Many people move too quickly from alternatives to conclusion, saying one option is better without identifying the dimension on which the comparison is being made. Deliberate language slows this movement by asking what is being compared, along which dimensions, and what differences actually matter. This chapter studies how alternatives are brought into a common frame, how dimensions of comparison are selected, and how differences are made explicit.
crumbs: Part II — Comparison, Trade-Off, and Evaluation

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
Informing
Asking
Directing








“The report is due tomorrow.”




“You need to finish it now.”





What is the speech trying to change?
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.2 Informing
Informing is the act of giving information to another person.
Its basic structure is:
Speaker → Information → Listener
The speaker changes the listener’s knowledge state.
Examples:

“The meeting starts at 3 p.m.”
“The file has been updated.”
“The client rejected the proposal.”
“The road is closed.”
“Your application has been approved.”

These utterances do not directly request action.
They provide information.
However, information often prepares action.
If someone says:

“The road is closed.”

The listener may infer:

“We need another route.”

If someone says:

“The client rejected the proposal.”

The listener may infer:

“We need to revise it.”

Information changes what action becomes reasonable.
This is why informing is not passive.
To inform is to alter the listener’s map of reality.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.3 The Responsibility of Informing
Informing carries responsibility because information affects judgment and action.
A speaker who informs must consider:

accuracy
completeness
relevance
timing
source
uncertainty
consequence

Bad informing can mislead even without direct lying.
For example:

“The project is fine.”

This may be technically true if no visible crisis has occurred. But if the speaker knows there are serious hidden risks, the statement may be pragmatically misleading.
A more responsible formulation would be:

“The project is still on schedule, but two dependencies are unresolved and may affect delivery next week.”

This statement separates:

current status
uncertainty
risk
time horizon

Good informing does not merely state a fact.
It gives the listener enough structure to understand what the fact means.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.4 Complete and Incomplete Information
Not all information is equally useful.
A weak informational statement may be true but insufficient.
Example:

“There is a problem with the system.”

This informs, but poorly.
The listener still does not know:

what system
what kind of problem
how serious it is
who is affected
whether action is required
how urgent it is

A stronger version:

“The payment system is rejecting card transactions from new users. Existing users are not affected. The issue started at 10:20 a.m., and engineering is investigating. We need a status update within one hour.”

This gives:

object
issue
scope
timing
affected group
current action
next expectation

Informing becomes stronger when it answers the listener’s likely needs.
A useful information act usually clarifies:
What happened?
Where did it happen?
Who is affected?
When did it happen?
How certain are we?
Why does it matter?
What happens next?
Not every situation requires all of these. But high-stakes informing usually requires more structure.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.5 Informing Without Directing
A key distinction:

Informing gives the listener knowledge.
Directing asks the listener to act.

These can be separated.
For example:

“The deadline is Friday.”

This informs.

“Please submit the draft by Friday.”

This directs.
The first changes awareness.
The second creates an expectation of action.
Confusion occurs when speakers inform but expect action.
For example:

“The trash is full.”

The speaker may intend:

“Please take out the trash.”

But the listener may treat it as information.
This can create frustration.
The speaker thinks the meaning was obvious.
The listener hears only a statement.
In low-stakes familiar relationships, indirect informing may work.
In high-stakes or operational situations, direct action language is usually better.
If action is required, say the action.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.6 Asking
Asking is the act of seeking information, confirmation, permission, judgment, or action from the listener.
Its basic structure is:
Speaker → Information Gap → Listener
The speaker presents a gap and invites the listener to fill it.
Examples:

“What time does the meeting start?”
“Did you send the file?”
“Why did the system fail?”
“Can we delay the launch?”
“What do you think of this plan?”

Asking is not only grammatical questioning.
It is a social act.
When a speaker asks, the listener is placed in a position of response.
This creates a small obligation.
The listener may answer, refuse, delay, clarify, redirect, or challenge the question.
Questions therefore shape interaction.
They decide what kind of information becomes relevant.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.7 Types of Asking
Not all questions seek the same kind of response.








Type
Function
Example




Factual Question
Seeks information
“When did this happen?”


Clarifying Question
Reduces ambiguity
“Do you mean the first version or the final version?”


Diagnostic Question
Searches for cause
“What changed before the error appeared?”


Evaluative Question
Seeks judgment
“Is this good enough for release?”


Permission Question
Seeks authorization
“Can I publish this?”


Preference Question
Seeks desire or choice
“Which option do you prefer?”


Challenge Question
Tests reasoning
“What evidence supports that conclusion?”


Reflective Question
Prompts thinking
“What are we assuming here?”


Rhetorical Question
Does not expect a real answer
“How could this possibly work?”



A good question makes clear what kind of answer is needed.
A vague question burdens the listener.
For example:

“Thoughts?”

This may be acceptable in casual conversation, but in many situations it is too open.
Better:

“Do you think the argument is clear enough for a non-technical reader?”

or:

“Can you check whether the second section supports the main claim?”

The better question defines the dimension of response.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.8 Asking as Attention Control
A question directs attention.
It tells the listener where to look.
Compare:

“Why did this fail?”

with:

“What condition made failure likely?”

The first may invite blame.
The second invites structural analysis.
Compare:

“Who is responsible?”

with:

“Which part of the process lacked ownership?”

The first searches for a person.
The second searches for a responsibility gap.
Compare:

“Do you like this?”

with:

“Does this solve the user’s problem?”

The first invites preference.
The second invites evaluation under criteria.
Questions are not neutral.
They shape the space of thought.
A strong question does not merely request information.
It creates a direction for inquiry.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.9 The Responsibility of Asking
Asking also carries responsibility.
A question can clarify, but it can also confuse, pressure, accuse, manipulate, or narrow thought.
For example:

“Why are you always late?”

This is formally a question, but pragmatically it contains accusation and generalization.
A better question:

“What is making it difficult to arrive by 9 a.m. consistently?”

This version still addresses the issue, but it changes the frame.
Another example:

“Don’t you think this plan is unrealistic?”

This question pushes the listener toward agreement.
A more open version:

“What risks do you see in this plan?”

Asking responsibly means designing questions that match the purpose of the conversation.
If the goal is inquiry, the question should keep inquiry open.
If the goal is decision, the question should focus criteria.
If the goal is accountability, the question should be specific.
If the goal is repair, the question should reduce defensiveness.
Bad questions often create bad thinking.
Good questions make better thinking possible.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.10 Asking Versus Requesting
A question can ask for information.
But it can also request action.
Consider:

“Can you send me the file?”

Literally, this asks about ability.
Pragmatically, it requests action.
The listener normally understands:

“Please send me the file.”

This shows that asking and directing often overlap.
A question form may soften a directive.
Compare:

“Send me the file.”

with:

“Can you send me the file?”

The second is less direct and more polite.
But the function is still directive.
This distinction matters because people often hide directions inside questions.
For example:

“Can you be more careful next time?”

This may be a request, criticism, warning, or instruction.
If the stakes are low, indirectness may be fine.
If the stakes are high, the speaker should make the action explicit:

“Next time, please verify the numbers against the source file before submitting the report.”

This reduces ambiguity.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.11 Directing
Directing is the act of moving someone toward action.
Its basic structure is:
Speaker → Desired Action → Listener
Examples:

“Please close the door.”
“Submit the report by 5 p.m.”
“Call the client before noon.”
“Do not publish this yet.”
“Use the revised version.”
“Focus on the first problem.”

Directing does not always appear as a command.
It may appear as:

request
suggestion
instruction
warning
recommendation
invitation
assignment
prohibition
reminder

For example:

“You might want to check the numbers again.”

This may function as gentle direction.

“The numbers need to be checked before this goes out.”

This directs through necessity.

“Do not send this until the numbers are checked.”

This directs explicitly.
Different forms carry different levels of force.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.12 Degrees of Directive Force
Directing can be soft or strong.



Form
Example
Force




Suggestion
“You might consider revising this.”
Low


Advice
“I think you should revise this.”
Moderate


Request
“Please revise this.”
Moderate


Instruction
“Revise this before submitting.”
Strong


Command
“Revise this now.”
Very strong


Prohibition
“Do not submit this version.”
Strong


Requirement
“This must be revised before approval.”
Very strong



The right degree depends on:

authority
urgency
relationship
stakes
listener competence
need for autonomy
risk of misunderstanding

Too much force can create resistance.
Too little force can create confusion.
For example, in a safety-critical situation:

“Maybe we should consider stopping the machine.”

This may be too weak.
Better:

“Stop the machine now.”

In a creative discussion:

“Change this immediately.”

may be too strong.
Better:

“This section may need a different structure. Can we explore another version?”

Directive force must fit the situation.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
section: 5.13 The Structure of a Good Direction
text:
5.13 The Structure of a Good Direction
A good direction usually includes:
Action
Object
Standard
Timing
Reason
Owner
Example:

“Please revise the introduction by making the problem statement more specific, and send the updated version by tomorrow afternoon so we can review it before the client meeting.”

This includes:

action: revise
object: introduction
standard: more specific problem statement
timing: tomorrow afternoon
reason: review before client meeting
owner: listener

A weak direction:

“Make it better.”

This is too vague.
The listener does not know:

what to change
where to change it
by when
according to what standard
for what purpose

Good directing reduces unnecessary interpretation.
It does not force the listener to guess the speaker’s intention.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.14 Direction and Authority
Directing always raises the question of authority.
Who has the right to direct whom?
Authority may come from:

role
expertise
ownership
responsibility
agreement
urgency
emergency
relationship
institutional position

A manager may direct an employee because of organizational role.
A doctor may direct a patient because of expertise.
A team member may direct another in a crisis because immediate coordination is needed.
A friend may make a request, but not command.
When authority is unclear, directive language can create tension.
For example:

“Do this by tomorrow.”

From a manager, it may be normal.
From a peer, it may sound controlling.
From a client, it may depend on contract and relationship.
From a stranger, it may be unacceptable.
Good directive speech respects the authority structure of the situation.
When authority is weak or shared, directions often need softer forms:
Could you take this part?
Would it make sense for you to handle the data check?
Can we agree that you will send the first draft by Friday?
I suggest we pause here and verify the assumption first.
The weaker the authority, the more the speaker must rely on agreement, reason, or negotiation.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.15 Directing Without Clarity
Many coordination failures happen because directions are unclear.
A speaker may think they have directed, when they have only hinted.
For example:

“This needs attention.”

This may mean:

fix it now
review it later
assign someone
monitor it
report back
ignore it unless it worsens

The phrase does not define the action.
A stronger version:

“Please review this section today, identify the source of the error, and tell me by 4 p.m. whether it can be fixed before release.”

This direction specifies action, object, deadline, and expected output.
Another vague direction:

“Be more proactive.”

Better:

“When you notice a blocker, please post it in the project channel the same day and propose one possible next step.”

The second version turns an abstract behavioral expectation into observable action.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.16 Informing, Asking, and Directing Compared
The three speech actions can be compared by what they attempt to change.









Speech Action
What It Changes
Basic Form
Listener Burden




Informing
Listener’s knowledge
“X is the case.”
Understand and update


Asking
Speaker’s knowledge or shared understanding
“What is X?”
Answer, clarify, or respond


Directing
Listener’s action
“Do X.”
Act, refuse, negotiate, or comply



The difference is not always grammatical.
A statement may direct.
A question may request.
A direction may inform.
The real test is function:
Is the speaker giving information?
Is the speaker seeking information?
Is the speaker moving the listener toward action?
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.17 Mixed Speech Acts
Many utterances combine informing, asking, and directing.
Example:

“The client meeting is tomorrow. Can you revise the summary tonight?”

This informs and directs through a question.
Example:

“The numbers look inconsistent. Could you check the source file?”

This informs and requests action.
Example:

“What changed in the pipeline, and please document the fix afterward.”

This asks and directs.
Mixed speech acts are common because real situations require multiple moves.
But when the stakes are high, the speaker should separate the functions clearly.
For example:
Information: The client found three errors in the report.
Question: Do we know where the errors came from?
Direction: Please trace the source, correct the numbers, and send me an update by 3 p.m.
Separating functions reduces confusion.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.18 Indirect Direction
People often direct indirectly.
They may say:

“It is noisy here.”

instead of:

“Please lower your voice.”

They may say:

“The deadline is close.”

instead of:

“You need to finish this today.”

They may say:

“This section is unclear.”

instead of:

“Rewrite this section.”

Indirect direction can be useful.
It may protect politeness, reduce pressure, or allow the listener to act voluntarily.
But indirect direction also risks misunderstanding.
A listener may not recognize that action is expected.
The speaker may then become frustrated:

“I already told you.”

But the speaker did not direct clearly.
A useful principle:

If action is required, make the action visible.

The more important the action, the less it should depend on inference.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.19 Refusal as Response to Direction
Directing creates the possibility of refusal.
A listener may say:

“I cannot do that.”
“Not by today.”
“I can do part of it.”
“I need more information first.”
“That is not my responsibility.”
“I disagree with that action.”

This does not necessarily mean the direction failed.
It may mean the direction entered negotiation.
A good directive act should allow reality to respond.
For example:

“Can you finish this by 5 p.m.?”

The listener may answer:

“I can finish the draft by 5, but not the final version.”

This response clarifies capacity.
Directive speech becomes stronger when it allows constraints to surface.
A bad direction demands compliance without checking feasibility.
A good direction creates action while still allowing clarification.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.20 Asking Before Directing
In uncertain situations, asking should often come before directing.
If the speaker lacks enough information, immediate direction may be premature.
For example:

“Fix the login bug now.”

This may be necessary in a crisis.
But if the situation is unclear, better:

“What do we know about the login bug? Who is affected? Is there a workaround? What is the fastest safe fix?”

After asking, direction can become more accurate:

“Given that only new users are affected and the workaround is available, prioritize diagnosis first and update support with the workaround message.”

Good leadership often moves through this sequence:
Ask → Understand → Direct
Bad leadership often skips the first two steps:
Assume → Direct
The result is misdirected action.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.21 Informing Before Asking
Sometimes informing should come before asking.
If the listener lacks context, a question may be confusing.
Weak version:

“What should we do?”

Stronger version:

“The launch is scheduled for Friday, but testing found two unresolved payment errors. Engineering says one can be fixed today, but the second may take three days. What should we do about the launch date?”

The second version informs before asking.
It gives the listener enough background to answer responsibly.
Good questions often require good informing first.
Without context, asking becomes a burden.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.22 Directing After Informing
Sometimes direction should follow information.
Weak version:

“Submit the revised proposal today.”

Stronger version:

“The client moved the review meeting to tomorrow morning, so we need the revised proposal today. Please update the pricing section and send it by 6 p.m.”

The information explains the direction.
This matters because reasons improve cooperation.
People are more likely to act well when they understand why the action matters.
A direction without reason may produce compliance.
A direction with reason can produce coordination.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.23 Failure Modes

5.23.1 Mistaking Information for Direction
Speaker:

“The room is messy.”

Intended meaning:

“Clean the room.”

Listener hears:

“The room is messy.”

The speaker expected action but gave information.


5.23.2 Asking a Question When Giving a Direction
Speaker:

“Can you try to be on time?”

Intended meaning:

“You must arrive on time.”

Listener hears:

“I should try, but it is flexible.”

The speaker used a weak question where a clear expectation was needed.


5.23.3 Directing Without Information
Speaker:

“Change the design.”

Listener asks:

“Why? Which part? According to what standard?”

The direction lacks context.


5.23.4 Informing Without Relevance
Speaker:

“There are many issues.”

Listener asks:

“Which issues matter? What should I do?”

The information is too general to support action.


5.23.5 Asking Without Scope
Speaker:

“What do you think?”

Listener asks:

“About what dimension?”

The question is too open.


5.23.6 Over-Directing
Speaker:

“Do exactly this.”

But the listener has more local knowledge.
Over-direction can suppress judgment and reduce adaptation.


5.23.7 Under-Directing
Speaker:

“Maybe we should look at this sometime.”

But action is urgent.
Under-direction can create delay and ambiguity.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.24 Practical Templates

5.24.1 Informing Template
The current situation is [fact/status].
This matters because [relevance/stakes].
The uncertainty is [unknown/condition], if any.
The next update/action is [next step], if needed.
Example:
The current situation is that the payment error affects new users only.
This matters because signups are dropping during onboarding.
The cause is still uncertain.
The next update will come after engineering checks the API logs.


5.24.2 Asking Template
I need to understand [information gap].
Specifically, can you clarify [focused question]?
This matters because [decision/action depending on answer].
Example:
I need to understand where the delay is coming from.
Specifically, can you clarify whether the blocker is design approval or backend implementation?
This matters because we need to decide whether to change scope or add support.


5.24.3 Directing Template
Please [action] on [object] by [time],
using [standard/method],
because [reason/stakes].
Tell me [expected response/output] when done.
Example:
Please revise the first section by tomorrow afternoon,
using the problem-goal-options structure,
because we need the chapter to align with the rest of the volume.
Tell me when the revised version is ready for review.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Informing, Asking, and Directing
text:
5.25 Minimal Operational Distinction
In real speech, a simple diagnostic is useful:
Informing: I want you to know X.
Asking: I want to know X from you.
Directing: I want you to do X.
This distinction prevents many communication failures.
Before speaking, ask:

Am I trying to inform, ask, or direct?

If the goal is knowledge transfer, inform clearly.
If the goal is information retrieval, ask precisely.
If the goal is action, direct responsibly.
Do not hide action inside vague information when clarity is needed.
Do not use questions to disguise commands when responsibility should be explicit.
Do not direct before asking when the situation is uncertain.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 5  Comparing Alternatives
text:
5.3 Chapter Summary
This chapter has studied how language compares alternatives under shared dimensions. Comparison is not judgment; it is the prior act of making differences visible. A common frame is necessary for meaningful comparison, and the selection of dimensions is itself an act of judgment. One-dimensional comparison is the most common failure, hiding dimensions that would complicate the picture. Dimensions can be operationalized into specific measures including cost, risk, reversibility, and strategic fit. Comparison tables provide a visual structure that makes differences explicit.

5.3.1 Essential Knowledge
The following principles must be retained and applied whenever you compare alternatives.
First, never compare alternatives without a common frame. Every option must be examined under the same set of dimensions, or the comparison is invalid. Second, resist the urge to compare on a single dimension, no matter how persuasive that dimension seems. A decision based on cost alone, or speed alone, or comfort alone, is almost always a mistake. Use at least three relevant dimensions. Third, choose your comparison dimensions deliberately. Dimensions are not neutral; they determine what becomes visible. If you are evaluating a career move, for example, comparing only salary and commute time while ignoring learning value and strategic fit produces a dangerously incomplete picture. Fourth, use time horizon as a dimension in every important comparison. An option that looks strong in year one may look weak in year five, and vice versa. Fifth, a comparison table is a tool for making differences explicit. When you disagree with someone about a decision, build a table together and see whether your disagreement is about facts, dimension weights, or the conclusion. These practices prevent shallow comparison from masquerading as reasoned judgment.
crumbs: Part II — Comparison, Trade-Off, and Evaluation

title: 6  Trade-Off and Competing Values
text:
6.1 When Good Things Cannot All Be Maximized
A trade-off appears when two or more valuable things cannot be fully preserved at the same time. This is one of the central realities of deliberate judgment. Many decisions are not difficult because one option is clearly good and another clearly bad; they are difficult because each option protects something worth protecting and sacrifices something that also matters.
The previous chapter studied comparison, which makes differences visible. This chapter moves one step deeper: once differences are visible, language must express the conflict between goals, values, benefits, costs, risks, and responsibilities that cannot all be maximized together. A simple decision asks which option is better; a trade-off asks what is being given up in exchange. Trade-off language has a basic form: choosing X gives us A but costs us B. Choosing a safer job gives more income stability but may reduce learning intensity; choosing speed gives faster delivery but may weaken quality.
crumbs: Part II — Comparison, Trade-Off, and Evaluation

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
“No.”




“I promise.”




“I will take care of it.”





Refusing
Promising
Committing



what they can ask from others
what others can expect from them
what has been accepted
what has been declined
what remains open
what is now binding
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.2 From Action to Obligation
Chapter 4 examined informing, asking, and directing.
Those speech acts move knowledge and action.
This chapter moves deeper into responsibility.
A direction says:

“Do this.”

A promise says:

“I will do this.”

A refusal says:

“I will not do this.”

A commitment says:

“You can rely on me for this.”

These are not minor differences.
They define who carries the burden of future action.
For example:

“Can you send the report tonight?”

This is a request.
The listener may answer:

“Yes, I will send it by 8 p.m.”

Now the speech situation has changed.
The listener has created an expectation.
The speaker can now plan around that expectation.
If the report is not sent, trust may be damaged.
A commitment is therefore not only a statement about future behavior.
It is a social bridge between present speech and future action.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.3 Refusing
Refusing is the speech act of rejecting a request, offer, expectation, role, obligation, or proposed action.
Its basic structure is:
Speaker → Boundary → Listener
A refusal says:

This will not be accepted.
This will not be done.
This will not continue.
This responsibility will not be taken.
This expectation will not be granted.

Examples:

“No.”
“I cannot do that.”
“I am not available tomorrow.”
“I cannot agree to these terms.”
“I will not take responsibility for that decision.”
“This is outside the scope of my role.”
“I appreciate the offer, but I have to decline.”

Refusal is one of the most important forms of pragmatic language because it protects boundaries.
A person who cannot refuse clearly will often accept responsibilities they cannot carry.
A team that cannot refuse scope will lose control of its work.
An organization that cannot refuse bad opportunities will lose strategic direction.
Refusal is not merely negative.
It is a structural act.
It preserves capacity, priority, integrity, and responsibility.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.4 What Refusal Does
A refusal performs several operations at once.
It rejects an expectation.
It defines a boundary.
It prevents false agreement.
It protects time, energy, money, attention, or values.
It may preserve trust by preventing overcommitment.
It may create disappointment, conflict, or negotiation.
For example:

“I cannot finish this by Friday.”

This refusal may disappoint the requester.
But if it is true, saying it clearly is more responsible than saying:

“I will try.”

when the speaker already knows the deadline is unrealistic.
A clear refusal may cause short-term discomfort.
An unclear non-refusal may cause long-term failure.
Refusal is therefore not the opposite of cooperation.
Often, refusal is what makes honest cooperation possible.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.5 Refusal and Boundary
Every refusal contains a boundary.
The boundary may concern:

time
role
capacity
authority
values
responsibility
scope
risk
consent
priority

Examples:

“I do not have time this week.”
“This is not my area of responsibility.”
“I cannot approve this without evidence.”
“I am not comfortable with that.”
“We cannot expand the scope without changing the deadline.”
“I cannot promise a result I do not control.”

A strong refusal makes the boundary visible.
A weak refusal leaves the boundary ambiguous.
For example:

“Maybe later.”

This may be polite, but it can create false expectation.
If the real meaning is no, a clearer version is better:

“I cannot take this on this month.”

or:

“I do not think I am the right person for this.”

The goal is not to be harsh.
The goal is to prevent the listener from building plans around a false possibility.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.6 Types of Refusal
Refusal can take different forms depending on the situation.








Type
Function
Example




Direct Refusal
Clearly rejects
“No, I cannot do that.”


Capacity Refusal
Refuses due to limited resources
“I do not have time this week.”


Scope Refusal
Refuses because request exceeds agreement
“That is outside the project scope.”


Authority Refusal
Refuses because speaker lacks power
“I cannot approve that decision.”


Value Refusal
Refuses because of principle
“I am not willing to do that.”


Risk Refusal
Refuses because consequence is too high
“We should not launch while this bug remains unresolved.”


Conditional Refusal
Refuses under current conditions
“Not unless the timeline changes.”


Redirecting Refusal
Refuses while offering alternative path
“I cannot review the whole draft, but I can review the introduction.”



These forms are not identical.
A direct refusal closes the path.
A conditional refusal leaves a possible path open.
A redirecting refusal preserves cooperation while limiting responsibility.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
section: 6.7 Refusal Is Not Always Rejection of the Person
text:
6.7 Refusal Is Not Always Rejection of the Person
One major difficulty in refusal is relational interpretation.
When someone hears:

“No.”

they may feel:

“You rejected me.”

But a refusal may reject only the request, not the person.
This distinction often needs to be made explicit.
For example:

“I value the project, but I cannot take on this extra section without delaying my current work.”

This separates:

respect for the project
refusal of the additional task
reason for refusal
consequence of accepting

Another example:

“I appreciate the invitation, but I cannot attend this time.”

This refuses the event, not the relationship.
In fragile relationships, refusal should often include relational care.
But relational care should not erase the boundary.
A refusal that sounds kind but remains unclear may fail as a refusal.
A good refusal can be both respectful and firm.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
section: 6.8 The Structure of a Responsible Refusal
text:
6.8 The Structure of a Responsible Refusal
A responsible refusal often contains five parts:
Recognition
Boundary
Reason
Alternative, if available
Closure

6.8.1 Recognition
Acknowledge the request or situation.

“I understand why this is important.”



6.8.2 Boundary
State the refusal clearly.

“I cannot take this on.”



6.8.3 Reason
Give enough explanation to make the refusal intelligible.

“My current deadline already requires full attention this week.”



6.8.4 Alternative
Offer another path only if it is real.

“I can review one section on Thursday, or we can ask someone else to take the full review.”



6.8.5 Closure
Prevent false ambiguity.

“But I cannot own the full task.”

A full example:

“I understand this review is urgent. I cannot take on the full document this week because my current delivery is already at capacity. I can review the introduction on Thursday, or we can find another reviewer for the full draft. But I cannot be responsible for the complete review.”

This refusal is clear, bounded, and cooperative.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.9 Bad Refusals
Refusal can fail in several ways.

6.9.1 The Hidden Refusal
The speaker intends to refuse but does not say so clearly.

“I will see.”
“Maybe.”
“Let me think about it.”
“I might be able to.”

These may be honest if uncertainty is real.
But if the speaker already knows the answer is no, these phrases create false expectation.


6.9.2 The Over-Explained Refusal
The speaker gives too much justification and weakens the boundary.

“I am so sorry, I really wanted to help, but there are many reasons, and I feel bad…”

Excess explanation may invite negotiation when the boundary is already fixed.


6.9.3 The Aggressive Refusal
The speaker protects the boundary but damages the relationship unnecessarily.

“That is not my problem.”

Sometimes this may be true, but it often creates avoidable conflict.


6.9.4 The Ambiguous Conditional Refusal
The speaker refuses under certain conditions but fails to define them.

“Not now.”

Does that mean later today, next week, next month, or never?
A clearer version:

“I cannot do this before Friday. If it can wait until next week, I can reconsider.”



6.9.5 The False Alternative
The speaker offers an alternative that is not real.

“Maybe someone else can help.”

This may sound cooperative but gives no actual path.
Better:

“I cannot help, and I do not know who is available.”

Honest limitation is better than decorative helpfulness.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.10 Promising
Promising is the speech act of creating expectation about future action.
Its basic structure is:
Speaker → Future Action → Listener
A promise says:

You may expect this from me.

Examples:

“I will call you tomorrow.”
“I promise I will be there.”
“We will deliver the report by Friday.”
“I will not share this information.”
“I will take responsibility for the next version.”

A promise binds speech to future behavior.
It changes what the listener may reasonably expect.
This is why promises are powerful.
They allow people to coordinate across time.
Without promises, cooperation would be fragile.
People could only respond to what is happening now. They could not reliably build plans around future action.
A promise creates continuity between present trust and future conduct.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.11 The Structure of a Promise
A strong promise usually includes:
Action
Owner
Time
Condition
Standard
Consequence
For example:

“I will send you the revised draft by 6 p.m. tomorrow, with the introduction rewritten and the evidence table updated.”

This promise defines:

owner: I
action: send the revised draft
time: by 6 p.m. tomorrow
standard: introduction rewritten, evidence table updated

A weak promise:

“I will handle it.”

This may be acceptable if the context is already shared.
But if context is unclear, it leaves too much undefined.
What does “handle” mean?
By when?
To what standard?
With what result?
Who will be informed?
A promise becomes stronger when the expected future is concrete enough to be checked.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.12 Promise and Trust
Promises create trust when they are fulfilled.
They damage trust when they are broken.
But the damage is not only caused by the missing action.
It is caused by the collapse of expectation.
If someone says:

“I will send it tonight.”

the listener may plan around that promise.
They may delay their own work.
They may tell others the task is covered.
They may stop searching for alternatives.
They may rely on the speaker.
If the promise fails, the harm includes all downstream dependencies.
This is why careless promising is dangerous.
A person may promise casually because the sentence feels small.
But the expectation created by the promise may become large.
Responsible promising requires awareness of dependency.
Before promising, ask:

Do I control the action?
Do I control the timing?
Do I understand the standard?
Do I have the capacity?
What will others rely on?
What happens if I fail?
Should this be a promise, an intention, or a possibility?

Not all future-oriented speech should become a promise.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.13 Promise, Intention, and Hope
A major pragmatic error is confusing promise with intention or hope.
These are different.








Form
Meaning
Example




Hope
Desired future, low control
“I hope to finish it soon.”


Intention
Planned future, some control
“I intend to finish it this week.”


Commitment
Accepted responsibility
“I will finish it by Friday.”


Promise
Strong interpersonal commitment
“I promise I will finish it by Friday.”



The difference matters.
If a speaker says:

“I hope I can help.”

the listener should not treat this as a promise.
If a speaker says:

“I will help.”

the listener may reasonably expect action.
If a speaker says:

“I promise I will help.”

the expectation becomes stronger, and failure becomes more serious.
A responsible speaker should choose the correct level of commitment.
Do not use promise language for uncertain possibilities.
Do not use vague intention when others need commitment.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.14 Commitment
Commitment is broader than promise.
A promise is usually made to someone.
A commitment may be made to a person, group, role, value, plan, goal, standard, or future course of action.
Examples:

“I will lead this workstream.”
“We are committed to finishing this project.”
“I take responsibility for the final review.”
“From now on, all releases must pass this checklist.”
“We will prioritize reliability over speed in this phase.”

Commitment organizes future behavior.
It tells others:

This is the direction we will hold.
This is the responsibility we accept.
This is the standard we will follow.
This is the role we will occupy.

Commitment is essential for coordination because it stabilizes expectation.
Without commitment, people may talk endlessly but never bind themselves to action.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.15 Commitment as Role Acceptance
Many commitments are role commitments.
To accept a role is to accept a bundle of expectations.
For example:

“I will be the project owner.”

This means more than:

“I will do some tasks.”

It may imply responsibility for:

progress
coordination
communication
risk tracking
decision escalation
delivery
accountability

If the role is not defined, the commitment is unstable.
A person may say:

“I will own this.”

But “own” may mean different things:

I will execute the task.
I will coordinate others.
I will make decisions.
I will report progress.
I will be accountable for the result.
I will make sure someone handles it.

A responsible commitment defines the role.
Better:

“I will own the final review. That means I will collect comments, resolve conflicts, check the final version, and send the approval note by Friday.”

This turns role language into operational commitment.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.16 Commitment as Boundary
Commitment does not only say yes.
It also says no to competing possibilities.
To commit to one path is to refuse other paths.
For example:

“We will prioritize quality over speed.”

This commitment means:

We will not sacrifice quality merely to move faster.

Another example:

“I will focus on IELTS preparation for the next three months.”

This commitment means:

I will reduce or reject activities that compete with that priority.

A commitment is meaningful only if it constrains future action.
If a commitment does not change what one will refuse, it is weak.
This is why commitment and refusal are structurally linked.
To commit is to accept one responsibility and reject others.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.17 Explicit and Implicit Commitment
Commitments can be explicit or implicit.
Explicit commitment:

“I will send the proposal by Friday.”

Implicit commitment:

The speaker repeatedly behaves as if they are responsible, and others begin to rely on them.

Implicit commitments are common in families, teams, friendships, and organizations.
For example, if one person always organizes the meeting notes, others may begin to assume:

“This person owns the notes.”

Even if no one said it directly.
Implicit commitment can support smooth coordination, but it can also create hidden burden.
A person may become responsible for something without ever agreeing to it.
This often causes resentment.
A useful pragmatic practice is to make implicit commitments explicit.
For example:

“It seems I have been handling the notes by default. Should I officially own this, or should we rotate the responsibility?”

This sentence exposes hidden responsibility.
It allows renegotiation.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.18 Conditional Commitment
Not all commitments are absolute.
Many responsible commitments are conditional.
Examples:

“I can finish this by Friday if I receive the data by Wednesday.”
“We can launch next week if testing passes.”
“I will join the project if my role is clearly defined.”
“I can support the plan if the budget risk is addressed.”

Conditional commitment is often stronger than vague agreement.
It clarifies what must be true for the commitment to hold.
A weak version:

“Yes, we can probably do it.”

A stronger version:

“We can do it by Friday if scope remains unchanged and design approval comes by Tuesday.”

This protects coordination.
It prevents people from treating a conditional possibility as an unconditional promise.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.19 Commitment and Capacity
A commitment is only responsible if it respects capacity.
Capacity includes:

time
attention
skill
authority
resources
emotional energy
decision power
control over dependencies

A person may sincerely want to commit but lack capacity.
For example:

“I will finish the whole project this week.”

This may be emotionally sincere but operationally false.
Good commitment requires capacity checking.
Before committing, ask:
What exactly am I accepting?
Do I control the required actions?
Do I have the time?
Do I have the skill?
Do I have the authority?
What dependencies could block me?
What standard must be met?
What happens if I cannot deliver?
If capacity is uncertain, use calibrated language:

“I can commit to completing the first draft by Friday. I cannot yet commit to the final version until I know how much revision will be required.”

This is more responsible than broad enthusiasm.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.20 Overcommitment
Overcommitment occurs when accepted responsibility exceeds real capacity.
It often comes from:

desire to please
fear of refusal
unclear scope
underestimating difficulty
pressure from authority
optimism bias
lack of boundary language
confusion between intention and commitment

Overcommitment damages trust because it creates expectations that cannot be fulfilled.
A person may believe they are being generous by saying yes.
But if the yes is unrealistic, it becomes a future failure.
A responsible speaker must sometimes refuse in order to protect real commitment.
For example:

“I cannot take on the additional chapter this week. If I accept it, I will risk delaying the current chapter. I can either finish the current chapter well, or switch priorities, but I cannot responsibly do both.”

This is not weakness.
It is accurate commitment management.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.21 Undercommitment
Undercommitment is the opposite failure.
It occurs when a person avoids responsibility even when commitment is needed.
Forms of undercommitment include:

“I will try.”
“Maybe.”
“Let us see.”
“I might be able to.”
“It should be fine.”
“Someone will handle it.”

Sometimes these phrases are appropriate because reality is uncertain.
But when the situation requires ownership, they are too weak.
For example, in a team setting:

“Someone should check the final numbers.”

This does not assign responsibility.
Better:

“I will check the final numbers by 4 p.m.”

or:

“Li will check the final numbers, and I will review them before sending.”

Undercommitment creates coordination gaps.
Everyone may assume someone else is responsible.
Clear commitment closes the gap.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.22 Broken Promises and Repair
Promises may fail.
Commitments may become impossible.
The question is not only whether failure happens, but how the speaker handles it.
A broken promise without repair damages trust deeply.
A responsible repair should include:
Acknowledgment
Responsibility
Explanation
Impact
Correction
Future prevention
Example:

“I said I would send the report by 6 p.m., and I did not. That created a problem for your review schedule. The delay happened because I underestimated the data cleanup, but that was my responsibility to communicate earlier. I can send the completed version by 10 a.m. tomorrow, and next time I will flag this kind of delay as soon as I see it.”

This repair does not hide behind excuses.
It names:

the original commitment
the failure
the impact
the cause
the responsibility
the correction
the prevention

A weak repair:

“Sorry, things got busy.”

This may be emotionally polite, but structurally insufficient.
When trust is damaged, repair must address the expectation that was broken.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.23 Refusal, Promise, and Commitment Compared
These three speech acts can be compared by how they manage responsibility.









Speech Act
Basic Function
Responsibility Effect
Example




Refusing
Rejects expectation or obligation
Limits responsibility
“I cannot do that.”


Promising
Creates expectation of future action
Accepts responsibility
“I will do that by Friday.”


Committing
Binds speaker to role, plan, value, or action
Stabilizes responsibility over time
“I will own this workstream.”



Refusal protects the boundary of responsibility.
Promise creates a specific expectation.
Commitment stabilizes a future pattern of responsibility.
All three are necessary.
Without refusal, responsibility expands without limit.
Without promise, future action cannot be trusted.
Without commitment, plans lack ownership.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.24 The Social Weight of Speech
Refusing, promising, and committing have social weight because they alter expectation.
After refusal, the listener must seek another path.
After promise, the listener may rely on the speaker.
After commitment, others may organize around the speaker’s role.
This is why these speech acts require care.
They affect:

trust
planning
coordination
emotional safety
role clarity
future accountability

A person who refuses carelessly may appear unreliable or cold.
A person who promises carelessly may appear trustworthy in the moment but unreliable over time.
A person who commits vaguely may create confusion while appearing cooperative.
Pragmatic maturity requires calibration.
Say no when the answer is no.
Say yes only when yes can be carried.
Define commitment when others must rely on it.
crumbs: Part II — Speech Action and Intention

title: 6  Refusing, Promising, and Committing
text:
6.25 The Grammar of Refusal
